coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive and label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on carbon dots-black phosphorus nanohybrid for the detection of Ochratoxins A
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kunxia
Qiao, Xiujuan
Zhao, Haiyan
He, Yaping
Sheng, Qinglin
Yue, Tianli
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have synthesized the novel carbon dots-black phosphorus nanohybrid (CDs-BP). And a novel label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on CDs-BP was constructed for ultrasensitive detection of Ochratoxins A (OTA). The morphology and structure of nanocomposite were analyzed by transmission electron microscope, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Zeta potential, et al. The CDs-BP nanohybrid, an excellent carrier for the immobilization of aptamer (Apta), was prepared through C-P bond, resulting in the enhancement of the electrochemical sensing performance for OTA detection. The results showed that the aptasensor had a wide linear range towards OTA (0.1 fg/mL − 10.0 ng/mL) with a low detection limit of 0.03 fg/mL under the optimal conditions. The proposed aptasensor displayed good electrochemical stability and excellent selectivity to OTA detection. Moreover, a satisfactory practical application results were obtained for different actual samples of wheat and grape juice samples. Thus, a new aptasensor was raised based on the CDs-BP nanocompound for the electrochemical detection of contaminants in edible agricultural food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical aptasensor
Carbon dots-black phosphorus
Label-free
Ultrasensitive
Ochratoxins A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of CDs and CDs-BP nanomaterial
The scheme of detection of OTA based on Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE
Fabrication and electrochemical characterization of the aptasensor
Electrochemical detection of OTA based on Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE
Specificity and repeatability of the proposed aptamer sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) BP NSs, (B, C) CDs-BP nanomaterial; (D) EDS spectrum of CDs-BP; (E) STEM image and element mapping of CDs-BP.

HRXPS C 1 s of CDs (A) and CDs-BP (B); HRXPS N 1 s of CDs (C) and CDs-BP (D); HRXPS S 2p spectra of CDs (E) and CDs-BP (F); HRXPS P 2p spectra of BP (G) and CDs-BP (H); UV–Vis spectra of the nanomaterials (I).
EIS curves of different electrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-: (a) GCE, (b) CDs-BP/GCE, (c) Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE, and (d) OTA/Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE.
(A) EIS curves of apta/CDs-BP/GCE with different OTA concentration (0.1 fg/mL − 10.0 ng/mL). (B) Linear relationship between △Ret/R0 et and logCOTA.
The study about specificity (A) and reproducibility (B) of Apta/CDs-BP/GCE sensor.
Illustration of the preparation of CDs-BP nanomaterials and its application in electrochemical sensing.
Compared with other sensors for the detection of OTA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 4-carboxyphenyl; b: microparticle beads; c screen-printed carbon electrodes; d horseradish peroxidase-labeled; e screen-printed gold electrode; f polythionine; g carboxylic porous carbon; h capture DNA

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive and label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on carbon dots-black phosphorus nanohybrid for the detection of Ochratoxins A

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Materials Science/Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry & Materials Science/Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education/Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Xi’an University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710065, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Technology, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kunxia Li and Xiujuan Qiao contribute equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have synthesized the novel carbon dots-black phosphorus nanohybrid (CDs-BP). And a novel label-free electrochemical aptasensor based on CDs-BP was constructed for ultrasensitive detection of Ochratoxins A (OTA). The morphology and structure of nanocomposite were analyzed by transmission electron microscope, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Zeta potential, et al. The CDs-BP nanohybrid, an excellent carrier for the immobilization of aptamer (Apta), was prepared through C-P bond, resulting in the enhancement of the electrochemical sensing performance for OTA detection. The results showed that the aptasensor had a wide linear range towards OTA (0.1 fg/mL − 10.0 ng/mL) with a low detection limit of 0.03 fg/mL under the optimal conditions. The proposed aptasensor displayed good electrochemical stability and excellent selectivity to OTA detection. Moreover, a satisfactory practical application results were obtained for different actual samples of wheat and grape juice samples. Thus, a new aptasensor was raised based on the CDs-BP nanocompound for the electrochemical detection of contaminants in edible agricultural food.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases are often caused by unclean foods or beverages which are always contaminated in the presence of viruses, bacteria or fungi [1]. And it occurs in unpredictable and different ways in developing and developed countries. Ochratoxins A (OTA) is a secondary metabolite from various toxigenic strains of the fungi Aspergillus and Penicillium. And it is the most common mycotoxin and the most toxic contamination in provisions around the world. It can be found in a series of food products, including coffee, dried fruits, spices, cereals and its derived products, and animal products [2,3]. Besides, OTA can cause many potential symptoms of acute and chronic, such as strong teratogenicity, carcinogenicity, hepatotoxicity, and immunotoxicity both human being and animals [4–6]. Hence, the International Agency of Research on Cancer has identified it as a potential carcinogen (Level 2B) in humans. Due to its potential toxicity, some regulatory agencies of the European Union have introduced the maximum level of OTA in food stuff (5 or 10 μg/kg) and beverages (2 μg/kg) (EC No. 123/2005). In view of these serious toxicity, it is of great significance to develop sensitive methods for the detection of OTA. Although high-performance liquid chromatography with fluorescence detection (HPLC-FLD) [7], solid-phase extraction (SPE) [8], enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9] have been widely used to detect OTA, these methods still have much limitations of time consuming, expensive equipment and large sample volumes. More recently, the biosensors for detecting OTA have arisen, mainly involving fluorescence [10], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [11], electrochemical biosensors [12,13]. Among them, the development of label-free electrochemical aptamer sensors (aptasensors) have been noticed due to their unique advantages of being simple, flexible, sensitive, and selective [14,15].

Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, especially 2D layered nanomaterials, have become the research foci. It has been applied in many fields, including drug delivery (platform for cancer chemotherapy) [16], chemical sensing (small molecules) [17,18], electronics and optoelectronics applications [19]. The layered 2D nanomaterials are mainly categorized into the following types: hexagonal ring with monoatomic layer represented by graphene, transition metal disulfides represented by molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and other layered 2D nanomaterials represented by black phosphorus (BP) [20]. They own high chemical activity and could withstand a large strain (≥25%) due to their ultrahigh surface volume ratio and strong flexibility in terms of mechanical properties [21–23], which leads them to become the foci of researchers. In the near future, BP especially will draw the interest of scientists among the big family of 2D nanomaterials because of its unique structure and properties. The pleated flaky phosphorus are formed through phosphorus atoms stacking together by weak interlayer van der Waals forces and strong interlayer P-P bonding between phosphorus atoms [16]. Based on its characteristics of ultrahigh surface volume ratio, high carrier mobility, ultra-thin 2D feature, and direct band gap [23], BP shows optical response and anisotropic electrical conductivity properties [24]. Carbon dots (CDs), as the quasi-zero-dimensional (the size in three dimensions is all less than or equal to 10 nm) carbon materials, have been widely investigated in the past decade because they have unique characteristics, such as low toxicity and low cost, simple synthesis, high chemical stability, and excellent biocompatibility. Besides, CDs are easily surface-passivated by a variety of organic compounds or biomolecules [25–27]. Thus, it has been employed in fields of bioimaging and biosensors interface materials, environmental monitoring, food quality control, and energy storage and conversion, and so on [28].

Due to these superior properties, we suppose that the combination of carbon dots and black phosphorus could enhance the performance of the electrochemical sensing platform. In this manuscript, we hence synthesized the chimeric carbon dots-black phosphorus nanohybrid (CDs-BP) with ultrahigh surface volume ratio. And CDs-BP had been used as biological carrier, which could provide more active sites for aptamer immobilization. Furthermore, a novel and efficient aptasensor based on CDs-BP nanocomposite was fabricated for the ultrasensitive determination of OTA by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).
In this work, the oligonucleotides of ochratoxins A were booked in Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences used are as follows:
5′-GATCGGGTGTGGGTGGCGTAAAGGGAGCATCGGACA-(CH2)6-3′,

Tri (Hydroxymethyl) amino methane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) was also purchased from Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). OTA, zearalenone (ZEA), black phosphorus solution, potassium ferrocyanide (K4Fe(CN)6), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), citric acid (CA) and thiourea were all purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China); Chitosan (MW: 5 ~ 6 × 105, >90% deacetylation) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanju Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 D). Wheat and grape juice were bought from the local market. All reagents used were analytical grade and no need to further purification. All solutions were prepared with double deionized water.

Field emission transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (FE-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, US) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) was used to acquire the morphologies of the BP NSs and P/CDs nanocomposites. UV–VIS spectra were obtained by UV-3600 Plus (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried on a PHI 5000 Versaprobe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Zeta potential was recorded by NanoPlus (Micromeritics Instrument Ltd., Shanghai, China). The charts of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were obtained through CHI 660 D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Co. Ltd., China). A traditional three-electrode system was used in this work. The modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a geometric area of 0.071 cm2 was used as the working electrode; and an Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum wire electrode were used as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. In addition, EIS measurement was performed (alternative voltage amplitude: 5 mV; the applied potential: 200 mV; and the voltage frequencies range: 0.01 Hz ~ 100 kHz). And the supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M PBS solution.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared according to the method in previously reported literature [29]. In this work, 1.05 g of citric acid and thiourea were added to the beaker and dissolved in 25.2 mL water; and then, the mixture solution was transferred into 40 mL Teflon-lined-reactor and carried out at 200 ℃ to synthesize carbon quantum dots. After the reaction lasting for 8 h, the light yellow aqueous solution with irritating smell was obtained when it naturally cooled to room temperature. After that, the above solution was dialysed in 500 D dialysis membrane lasting 3 days to remove impurities and reach CDs.

In this step, CDs-BP composite was synthesized by simple in situ polymerization. Specifically, 2.0 mg/mL of nitrogen saturated black phosphorus solution was mixed with 4 mg/ml of carbon quantum dot solution in an equal volume and ultra-sonicated for 30 min. The dark grey solution of CDs-BP nanocomposite was obtained. It is worth noting that the nanocomposite needs to be protected from light at 4 ℃.

The glass carbon electrode (GCE) was pre-treatment before modification with nonohybride. Firstly, the GCE was mechanically polished by 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina power to obtain mirror-like surface, respectively. And then the GCE was cleaned by ultra-sonication in the mixture of distilled water and ethanol. Next, the GCE was allowed to dry in a stream of purity nitrogen. 1.0 mg CDs-BP nanocomposite was disperse‘d into 1 mL chitosan (W/V = 0.5%) and ultra-sonicated for 10 min. Finally, 7 μL sample nanocomposite dispersion was dropped onto the mirror-like surface of GCE, and then dried in ambient air to form the modified electrodes.

After drying in the ambient air, the CDs-BP/GCE was incubated in a 0.5 μM (pH 7.4) aptamer mixture that containing 1.0 μM signal molecule modified aptamer, 0.1 mM TCEP and 10 mM Tris-HCl for 12 h at room temperature. Then, electrode modified by aptamer nanoconjugates (Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE) was washed by ultrapure water and dried under a moderate nitrogen stream. Finally, electrode modified by aptamer nanoconjugates was incubated in a range of concentrations of OTA for further detection.

Briefly, 10 g wheat samples were milled in a food mixer. Wheat sample powder was then extracted by 40 mL the mixed solution of methanol–water (60:40, v/v,) and orbitally shaken for 5 min to obtain the suspension. The suspension was filtered through a filter paper to get the extraction with slight opalescent yellow color.
Grape juice samples were obtained from a local market. 10 mL grape juice aliquot was adjusted to pH (1.5) by 1.0 M HCl for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CDs and CDs-BP nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to characterize these nanomaterials surface morphology, and the results were shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM images of black phosphorus nanosheets (BP NSs) (Fig. 1A) and CDs-BP (Fig. 1B, 1C) could be observed. CDs were uniformly dispersed on the black phosphorus nanosheets substrate. Besides, the Zeta potential of nanomaterials were also ascertained. CDs have positive charges due to protonation of the amino group, while BP NSs have negative charges which well interpreted the uniform distribution of carbon dots on the black phosphorus substrate.

CDs-BP nanomaterial’s elements were further studied by EDX. The elements of phosphorus, carbon and nitrogen could be found in CDs-BP nanohybride (Fig. 1D). Besides, all the elements of C and, P and N in CDs-BP nanohybride covered the whole composite and uniformly distributed on CDs-BP nanohybride surface (Fig. 1E, Red color represents C, blue color represents P and green color represents N). The results proved that the BP NSs were covered by uniform distribution of carbon quantum dots.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was utilized to discuss the chemical states and surface compositions of the elements in these prepared nanomaterials. The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HRXPS) spectrum of C1s, N1s, S2p and P2p of these nanomaterials were analyzed in Fig. 2 A − 2H. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of CDs could be fitted with three peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.2 eV, corresponding to C–C/C = C, C-N/C-O and C = N/C = O bond, respectively (Fig. 2A) [30]. For CDs-BP, in addition to the above three functional groups that are slightly shifted to the left by 0.3 eV, it also contains a C-P bond at 283.6 eV [31], indicating that the carbon quantum dots are successfully combined with the black phosphorus nanosheets, which is consistent with the measurement results in the previous section (Fig. 2B). There are three peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.7 eV, and 401.2 eV in N1s spectrum of CDs, attributing to C = N, C-N-C, and N–H bonds (Fig. 2C) [32]. Compared with CDs nanomaterials, these functional groups of CDs-BP are slightly shifted to the left by about 0.5 eV (Fig. 2D). The presumed reason for this phenomenon is the electrostatic interaction between carbon dots and black phosphorus nanosheets. Based on the above analysis, the HRXPS of S2p in CDs showed doublet peaks located at 162.2 eV and 163.3 eV, which were ascribed to C-S2p 3/2 and C-S2p 1/2 (Fig. 2E) [33,34]. Similar to N1s, S2p bonds in CDs-BP also slightly shifted to the left by 0.3 eV (Fig. 2F). By comparing the XPS spectra of the pre-nanocomposites and post-nanocomposites, it was found that the absorption peak of the carbon, nitrogen and sulfur elements in the nanocomposites are red-shifted due to the introduction of phosphorus containing unbonded electrons in the nanocomposites. For single BP NSs, three fitted peaks located at 129.4 eV, 130.4 eV, and 134.6 eV are the P2p 3/2 , P2p 1/2 , and P-Ox bonds, respectively. Among of the three peaks, P2p 3/2 and P2p 1/2 are orbitals of zero-valent state P in the P2p spectrum. Another relatively weak peak was produced by the slight oxidation of the black phosphorus nanosheets (Fig. 2G) [18]. In CDs-BP nanomaterials, there was a new P-C bond at 132.1 eV in addition to above three bonds, which indicates a successful combination of carbon dots and black phosphorus nanosheets (Fig. 2H) [31].

The absorbance spectra of the BP NSs, CDs, and CDs-BP dispersion were measured by means of UV–Vis spectroscopy. Some obvious absorption peaks were displayed in Fig. 2I. For separate CDs, there was a wide and weak absorption peak at 283.2 nm, which could be ascribed to the π-π* transition of C = C bond [35]. The strongly absorption peak at 328.9 nm was relevant to n-π* transition of C = N/C = O [36]. The characteristic peak of BP NSs at 347.4 nm was clearly shown in near UV region [18]. For CDs-BP nanocomposites, a moderately strong absorption peak at 330.5 nm was presented on the UV–Vis spectrum, which might be due to the neutralization of absorption of black phosphorus and carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The scheme of detection of OTA based on Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of aptamers to bind with a great variety of targets with high affinity and specificity comparable to that of antibodies makes them promising molecular receptors for bioanalytical applications [37]. OTA aptamers have a specific ability to recognize OTA for analysis [38]. CDs are chemically inert and hydrophobic, so that the adsorption behavior between CDs and aptamer probes can be mainly determined by π-π stacking interactions and strong hydrophobic [39–41]. In this manuscript, the construction process and application principle of the aptamer sensor are illustrated in the Scheme 1 . First of all, the CDs-BP nanocomposites were fastened on the surface of the treated electrode. Then, the electrode modified with CDs-BP nanocomposites was incubated in 0.5 μM aptamer mixture solution to construct an aptamer sensor. After assembling the aptamer on CDs-BP/GCE, aptamers lied on the surface of CDs-BP nanocomposite through π-π stacking with unfolded stretch state. When the target of OTA was added into the sensing system, specific binding was performed based on the high recognition ability between the aptamer and the OTA. At the same time, the strong combination between OTA and aptamer results in the release aptamer from the nanocomposite surfaces, which contributes to the decrease in impedance value. Specifically, after the combination of OTA and aptamer, the release for aptamers form modified GCE occurred and EIS value decreased simultaneously. The method of detecting OTA on the basis of the change of impedance value in the sensing process was realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and electrochemical characterization of the aptasensor

ce:section.ce:para:
In order to obtain the desired results and the practicality of the proposed sensor, the incubation time of aptamer electrode and the pH of supporting electrolyte were studied. The incubation time of aptamer in OTA is one of the important indicators to determine whether the aptamer sensor has practical application value. Therefore, we studied the appropriate time for electrode incubation in 0.1 ng/mL OTA, and the results were shown in Fig. S1A. The Re
t of the aptamer sensor decreased significantly as the incubation time going in the early stage up to 13 min. After that, the Ret was gradually gently decreased due to the combination of apt and OTA tended to saturation. Consequently, 13 min was considered as optimum incubation time for the further research.

The pH of supporting electrolyte has an effect on the loading of the aptamer on the electrode surface in the system, and thereby affecting the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensor. As presented in Fig. S1B, the Ret obviously decreased in the range of pH 6.0 and pH 7.5, and then its value increased from pH 7.5 to pH 8.0. The impedance reached maximum when the pH value increased to 7.5, which indicated that the amount of aptamer loading on the electrode surface reached saturation. Whereas, considering that OTA is a contaminant in foods, pH 7.4 was chosen as the appropriate pH value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of OTA based on Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
EIS technique was applied for detecting OTA using the constructed aptamer sensor under optimal conditions in view of the change of electrochemical impedance at different concentrations. EIS measurement was implemented in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 M K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the △Ret (Ri et -R0 et ) increased as the OTA concentrations from 0.1 fg/mL to10.0 ng/mL (curve a to j). The interaction of OTA and the aptamer is close to saturation when OTA concentration is relatively high, assuming that this combination process meets the requirements of the Langmuir isotherm [43]. The binding constant (i.e., equilibrium constant, Kb ) can be expressed as equation (1). In the mathematical equation (1), Ri et and R0 et represented the resistance of charge transfer with certain concentration of OTA and without OTA, respectively. Therefore, a linear relationship between △Ret and OTA concentration (0.1 fg/mL and 10.0 ng/mL) was found (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation is as follow (2), and its correlation coefficient is 0.9974. And the detection limit was 0.03 fg/mL of the Apta/CDs-BP/GCE sensor. The related performance results were compared with previously reported electrochemical biosensors, shown in Table 1 , which showed that the aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE sensor was superior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and repeatability of the proposed aptamer sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity and reproducibility of the Aptamer/CDs-BP/GCE sensor were evaluated, respectively. First of all, its specificity was assessed by incubating the aptamer sensor in 0.1 ng/mL OTA, zearalenone (ZEA), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), and their mixture (0.1 ng/mL), respectively. The reason for choosing these potentially interfering mycotoxins is that they coexist with OTA in plants and foods. As shown in Fig. 5 A, we can clearly see that the aptasensor has the specific recognition ability to OTA. At the same time, its reproducibility is also assessed by five parallel electrodes and these results are shown in Fig. 5B. The impedance response results of the five parallel electrodes have been recorded in the sensing system, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is calculated to be 0.91%, which means that the aptamer sensor has a good reproducibility.

The storage stability test for the aptasensor under the applied potential has been studied by EIS technique. The proposed aptasensor is well recorded by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 1 mM redox probe [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- for the test of storage stability for 15 days. The result suggested that this aptasensor based on CDs-BP showed an excellent stability (Fig. S2).

In order to confirm the feasibility and reliability of the aptamer sensing system in the actual sample, the detection of OTA in wheat and grape juice was carried out. Firstly, the matrix calibration and the standard calibration have been evaluated. The relative deviations between the two calibration curves and the true values were all less than 8%. However, compared with standard calibration, the matrix calibration result has little relative deviation from the true value, indicating that the matrix calibration is better than standard calibration. The detection results were obtained by the prepared aptasensor and commercialized ELISA kit, respectively. The regression equation was obtained by these two kinds of methods: y = 0.9838x + 0.0889 (R = 0.9985, x axis: the prepared aptasensor; y axis: the commercialized ELISA kit). As shown in the regression equation, the slope between these two methods was about the ideal ‘1′, which illustrates that the prepared aptasensor could be preliminarily applied in OTA detection in foodstuff. According to the Commission Regulation (EC) No. 1661/2006, the maximum amount of OTA in crude cereals and grape juice are 5 ng/mL and 2 ng/mL respectively, indicating the proposed aptamer sensor can be used to detect contaminant OTA (Table S1).

In this work, a novel nanostructurated composites were synthesized by two steps method and an electrochemical sensing platform based on CDs-BP nanomaterials was presented. More importantly, the sensing platform provides functional assembly and signal amplification strategies to improve the performance of modified electrode and the sensitivity of aptamer sensors. Additionally, the assembly process of the aptasensor and the determination of OTA were discussed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Hence, the aptamer sensor based on CDs-BP nanomaterials presents a wide linear range from 0.1 fg/mL to 10.0 ng/mL for OTA detection under optimal conditions with a low limit of detection (0.03 fg/mL) for label-free impedimetric detection of OTA, and it had a good reproducibility. Moreover, the nanomaterials of CDs-BP represented the novelty of the substrate of the sensing platform that may cause concern in food safety and other application fields due to its some advantages of novelty and low cost.

Kunxia Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiujuan Qiao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - review. Haiyan Zhao: Visualization, Writing - review, Data curation. Yaping He: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Qinglin Sheng: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - editing. Tianli Yue: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this project by the National Key R&D Program of China (2019YFC1606703), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province in China (2020JM-429), National Science Foundation of Shaanxi province for new young star of science and technology (No. 2018KJXX-090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106378.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial-based sensors for detection of foodborne bacterial pathogens and toxins as well as pork adulteration in meat products
J. Food Drug Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Stephen Inbaraj, B. Chen, Nanomaterial-based sensors for detection of foodborne bacterial pathogens and toxins as well as pork adulteration in meat products, J. Food Drug Anal. 24 (2016) 15-28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrically addressable deposition of diazonium-functionalized antibodies on boron-doped diamond microcells for the detection of ochratoxin A
Anal. Methods.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Chrouda, A. Sbartai, F. Bessueille, L. Renaud, A. Maaref, N. Jaffrezic-Renault, Electrically addressable deposition of diazonium-functionalized antibodies on boron-doped diamond microcells for the detection of ochratoxin A. Anal. Methods. 7 (2015) 2444-2451.

Sens. Actuators, B.
